segments.s624-g9: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate content/purpose clause ('that'), so تا + clause = 'that ...'. خلعتش is خلعت (robe of honour) with the enclitic -ش 'his' attached after the noun. ساختند is the past plural 'they made/constructed' and completes the clause: 'he commanded that they made his robe.' Word order is classical Persian: verb of the main clause (بفرمود) + تا + subordinate clause (object + verb).
segments.s624-g10: گرانمایه گنجی is a noun phrase 'a precious/valuable treasure' (گرانمایه modifying گنجی) serving as the object; in Persian the object can precede the verb. بپرداختند is the past plural 'they paid/disbursed' (ب‑ as a classical/poetic prefix plus پرداختند), so the whole segment reads 'they paid a precious treasure.'
segments.s624-g11: بدو گفت is inverted order 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him/unto it'); گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses do not list the simple past meaning, so the actual sense is not in the supplied list). یزدان پناه تو باد is a blessing formula: یزدان پناه = 'God [be] refuge/protector', تو is the pronoun 'you', and باد is the optative particle 'may it be'. Together it yields 'May God be your refuge.'
segments.s624-g12: This is another optative blessing. سر تخت خورشید گاه is a noun phrase 'the top/head of the throne of the sun (i.e. a very high place)'; تو is 'you' and باد is the optative 'may it be'. The structure is nominal phrase + pronoun + optative particle, giving 'May you be at the head/top of the sun's throne (at all times).' Word order places the descriptive phrase before the verb-like optative باد.
segments.s624-g13: به + infinitive/noun here marks the temporal circumstance 'on/upon going' (به رفتن = 'on setting out'). دو هفته درنگ is a duration phrase 'a delay/pause of two weeks'. آمدش is the past 'came to him' (آمد + enclitic -ش), used impersonally here to mean 'befell him' or 'came upon him'. The whole means 'upon setting out, a delay of two weeks came upon him.'
segments.s624-g14: تن‌آسان خراسان is a nominative phrase 'the soft‑bodied/luxurious one of Khorasan' (تن‌آسان modifies the region/name). به چنگ آمدش is an idiomatic past construction: به + چنگ + آمدش = 'came into the clutches/hand of' meaning 'was seized' or 'fell into captivity'. Note the preposition به + noun + آمدش forming a passive/affected result 'came to/befell him.'
segments.s624-g15: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. نرسی (from رسیدن) is used here with the sense 'to arrive' (the clause means 'when he had not arrived / when the arrival did not come'), and بشد is an archaic/perfective auxiliary meaning 'it happened / it became' (here functioning to set up the ensuing result). هفته‌ای برگذشت is the main clause 'a week passed by.' Together: 'When he had not arrived, it came to pass that a week passed.'
segments.s624-g16: دل شاه is the subject 'the king's heart'. ز marks cause 'from/because of', so ز اندیشه = 'because of thought'. پردخته گشت uses گشت (past/become) to mean 'became' and پردخته here describes the state caused by thought (in context 'distressed/affected by thought'); the supplied senses do not list an exact 'distressed' sense for پردخته, so that lexeme's contextual meaning is given instead. Overall: 'the king's heart became troubled by thought.'
segments.s624-g33: همه + کهترانش = “all his inferiors”: همه is a universal quantifier modifying کهترانش; کهترانش is the plural noun 'inferiors' with the 3rd-person possessive suffix -ش ('his'). به کردار میش is a verbless simile: به + noun phrase (کردار = 'conduct/behaviour') + noun (میش = 'ewe') yields 'in the manner/behaviour of an ewe' (به is used adverbially = 'in/by').
segments.s624-g34: که introduces a relative/temporal clause 'such that / when'; روز شکارش = 'the day of his hunt' (شکار with the possessive -ش). سگ آید به پیش: آید is 3rd-person present/subjunctive used here with iterative/habitual sense 'would/comes', and the small prepositional sequence آید به پیش = 'comes before/in the presence of' (به + پیش).
segments.s624-g35: به کندی و تندی = 'by/with slowness and swiftness' where به makes the nouns adverbial ('by/according to'); بما ننگرید is a negative imperative/jussive addressed to the addressee(s) ('do not regard/consider us'); ننگرید is the prohibitive/imperative form (2nd-person plural/formal).
segments.s624-g36: وزین here is the fused poetic form (و + زین) meaning roughly 'and in/of this' (classical poetry often fuses و + demonstrative), followed by مرز 'realm/boundary'. کس را marks the object with را ('any person'), and به کس نشمرید is another negative imperative/jussive 'do not reckon/compare one person to another' (نشمرید = do not count/consider).
segments.s624-g37: Word order is verb before subject (typical of epic poetry): چنین گفت = 'said thus', and به موبد = 'to the mobed' (دative/indirect object precedes). گفت is a past-tense reporting verb ('said'); the subject بهرام گور follows it for emphasis (بهرام + epithet گور 'Gūr' = onager/epithet).
segments.s624-g38: که is the complementizer introducing reported speech 'that'. یزدان دهد is a jussive/optative use of دهد = 'may God give' (a prayer/wish); the items فر, دیهیم and زور are the three things requested/mentioned (فر = royal glory; دیهیم in this context refers to a rural/domainal possession or village-based authority; زور = strength/power).
segments.s624-g39: مراگر is an archaic fused conditional 'if (to) me' (مرا + گر); it introduces the protasis. جهاندار 'world-ruler/sovereign' is the agent and پیروز is a predicate/adjective 'victorious'; کرد is the past tense verb 'made/did' — together 'if the sovereign made me victorious'.
segments.s624-g40: شب تیره 'dark night' is the theme; بر بخت من = 'upon my fortune' (بر + noun + 1st-person possessive), and روز کرد uses کردن + روز = 'made into day' (کرد here = 'made/turned into'). The clause therefore says he changed the dark night of my fate into day.
segments.s624-g41: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral one) introducing a person; the two nominal phrases 'قیصر روم' and 'قیصر نژاد' are coordinate titles ('a Caesar of Rome' and 'a Caesar of the lineage'), linked by و. Note the poetic compression: the genitive relation in 'قیصر نژاد فریدون' that follows (next segment) is presented without the ezafe.
segments.s624-g42: This is the verb phrase: 'فریدون ورا تاج بر سر نهاد' = 'placed the crown on his head.' 'فریدون' here names the lineage (attached to the previous 'نژاد'); and ورا is the indirect object 'to him.' تاج بر سر is the prepositional object ('the crown upon the head'), with the usual Persian order (object before verb). نهاد is the past tense of نهادن ('put/placed'), a common archaic/poetic past form.
segments.s624-g43: بزرگست is an archaic/poetic form of 'بزرگ است' ('is great'); the copula is contracted. وز here functions as a poetic variant of 'از' ('from/of') indicating origin (see note), and سلم is the personal name Salm (one of Fereydun's sons) — the phrase means 'his lineage comes from Salm.' دارد + نژاد is a verbal construction meaning 'has (a) lineage' (نژاد follows as the object), the noun–verb order showing poetic inversion.
segments.s624-g44: فزون‌تر is the comparative 'more/greater.' ز شاهان = 'than the kings' (ز = 'from/than' here in poetic comparison). به + noun (به رسم و به داد) forms adverbial phrases 'in/with regard to ceremony and justice' (به marking the domain or manner). The coordination و links رسم ('custom/ceremony') and داد ('justice').
segments.s624-g45: کنون = 'now' (temporal). مردمی کرد is a light-verb construction where the adjective/noun مردمی ('popular, of the people') is the complement and کرد ('made/did') is the verb: 'made him a man of the people' / 'made him popular.' The following و فرزانگی continues the coordinated complements; the verb is understood to govern both (elliptical repetition of کرد).
segments.s624-g46: چو here means 'as'/'like' (introducing a simile) and is followed by خاقان ('khaghan, supreme ruler'). نیامد is the simple past negative 'did not come' (literally 'did not come into'). به + دیوانگی = 'into madness'; together the clause says he did not, like a khaghan, come into madness (i.e., he did not fall into madness as some rulers do).
segments.s624-g47: ورا پیش خوانیم is a verbal clause 'we will call him forward' — ورا is the clitic/object 'him,' پیش = 'before/in the presence (of us),' and خوانیم (1st-person plural present/future form of خواندن) here means 'we call/summon' (poetic use). هنگام بار = 'at the time/occasion' (هنگام = 'at the time of', بار = 'occasion/burden' used poetically for 'occasion').
segments.s624-g48: سخن = 'speech/words' (object domain). تا here introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that' and چه گوید = 'what he will say' (چه = what; گوید is the verb 'say' — note the lexeme's sense list in the input was nouny/mislabelled, but here it functions as the verb). که آید به کار is a relative/result clause 'that it may come to use' where آید = 'come' and به کار = 'to use / to be of service'; the sequence expresses 'to see what he will say that might be useful.'
segments.s624-g25: This starts with the indefinite 'یکی' (a certain / there was a), followed by the noun 'مرد' (man). 'پیرست' is an archaic fused form equivalent to 'پیر است' (is old) — so the clause means 'there was an old man'. The prepositional phrase 'با رای و شرم' (with counsel and modesty) gives two accompanying qualities: 'با' + noun(s) lists attributes of that man.
segments.s624-g26: 'سخن گفتنش' is a nominalized possessive: 'سخن' (speech) + 'گفتن' with the possessive/nominalizing clitic '-ش' = 'his saying' → 'his speech'. In classical/poetic Persian the copula is often omitted, so 'چرب' (flattering/smooth) is the predicative adjective of 'سخن گفتنش'. The conjunction 'و آواز نرم' links a second predicative phrase: 'آواز' (voice) described by 'نرم' (soft).
segments.s624-g27: The phrase is elliptical and poetic. 'کسی' (someone/a certain one) is the indefinite subject. Here 'کش' functions as an agentive/relational element tied to the following object 'فلاطون' (Plato). 'فلاطون به دست اوستاد' literally reads 'Plato, by his hand, (was) master' — i.e. the man is presented as someone who had Plato in his hand as a pupil or whom Plato associated with; the copula is omitted and 'به دست' marks the agent/relationship ('by/at the hand of').
segments.s624-g28: This segment lists adjectival qualities that modify the preceding 'کسی'. 'خردمند' (wise) is the first adjective; the second coordinate 'بادانش' is formed from 'با + دانش' and means 'possessing knowledge / learned' (a compound adjective). The final 'بانژاد' is another compound meaning 'of good / noble birth'. Adjectives follow the noun and are coordinated by 'و'.
segments.s624-g29: 'یکی' (a certain one) introduces another person. 'برمنش' is an adjectival compound meaning 'of good / noble manners' and functions as predicate with the copula 'بود' (was). The following 'کامد ز روم' uses the archaic past participle/verb 'کامد' (came/arrived) together with 'بود' (literally 'was come'), forming a past/perfect sense: 'had come from Rome' ('ز' = from, 'روم' = Rome/Byzantium).
segments.s624-g30: 'کنون' (now) is a temporal adverb. 'خیره گشت' is a light-verb construction meaning 'became astonished / stood staring' (خیره = astonished/staring; گشت = became). 'اندرین مرز و بوم' is the locative phrase 'in this border and land' (اندرین = in these/this; مرز و بوم = border and homeland), giving the place where he stood astonished.
segments.s624-g31: 'بپژمرد' is a past/preterite verb meaning 'he withered' (the prefix ب- plus پژمرد giving a past sense in poetry). 'چون' introduces a simile ('like'), followed by 'لاله' (tulip) as the image. 'در ماه دی' is a temporal phrase ('in the month Dey' — a winter month); note 'دی' here names the month, so the whole line compares his decline to a tulip withering in midwinter.
segments.s624-g32: 'تنش' = 'his body' (تن + possessive clitic -ش). 'خشک' is a predicative adjective describing the body ('dry', i.e., withered/pale). The coordinated phrase 'رخساره همرنگ نی' describes his face/complexion: 'رخساره' (face/complexion) + 'همرنگ' (the same color as) + 'نی' (reed) — a poetic simile meaning pale/yellowish like a reed. Again the copula is omitted; adjectives directly follow their nouns.
segments.s624-g1: The phrase is inverted: the indirect object 'به نرسی' (to Nersi) comes first, followed by 'چنین گفت' (said thus) and the temporal/agent phrase 'یک روز شاه' (one day the king). In idiomatic modern order it would be 'یک روز شاه چنین به نرسی گفت' — the verb 'گفت' is the past 'said'.
segments.s624-g2: کز + ایدر here gives a locative/ablative sense 'from here' (کز = 'from which / from here'); 'برو' is the imperative 'go'; 'با' marks accompaniment 'with' and links the two nouns 'نگین و کلاه' ('a gem and a hat').
segments.s624-g3: The clause places the place-name 'خراسان' before the verb; 'ت' + 'را' together mark the second-person target 'to you' (here split as clitic + object marker). 'دادم' is first-person past 'I gave'. 'آباد کن' is an imperative 'make [it] prosperous' directed at the addressee.
segments.s624-g4: 'دل زیردستان' is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the hearts of the subjects'; 'به ما' functions as 'for us' / 'for our sake'; 'شاد کن' is another imperative 'make [them] joyful.' The two imperatives are coordinated as instructions from the speaker.
segments.s624-g5: 'نگر' is an imperative 'see to it / take care'; 'تا' introduces the content or purpose 'that/so that'; 'نباشی' is a subjunctive/negative form of 'بودن' used here in the sense 'be not' (i.e. 'be nothing except'); 'به جز دادگر' means 'except a just one' — together: 'Make sure you are nothing but a just man.'
segments.s624-g6: میاویز is the imperfective/present-form (mi‑ prefix) of 'آویختن' meaning 'hang' and here gives an instruction about action to avoid or not commit; 'چنگ' (harp) is the direct object, and 'اندرین رهگذر' means 'among these passersby' (اندرین = 'in/among these'). The clause functions as a figurative prohibition: do not hang the harp on passing people.
segments.s624-g7: 'پدر کرد بیداد' literally 'the father did injustice' (کرد + noun object): 'کرد' is the past tense 'did / committed'. 'و پیچد ازان' uses a poetic/older form 'پیچد' (= 'پیچید' / 'turned') plus 'ازان' 'from that/thereupon', so 'and he turned away from it.'
segments.s624-g8: چو is the simile marker 'like/as'; 'مردی' here means 'a man'; 'برهنه' 'bare/exposed' describes him; 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' and 'باد خزان' 'the autumn wind' is the agent leaving him exposed — 'like a man stripped by the autumn wind.'
segments.s624-g17: بفرمود تا is a classical, polite/periphrastic command: بفرمود (honorific past 'he ordered/said') + تا introduces the subordinate purpose/command clause ('that ...'). موبد موبدان is a titular compound: موبد (mōbed, 'priest') followed by موبدان (the priests) to mean 'the chief of the priests' (a title expressed by juxtaposing the two nouns).
segments.s624-g18: برفت و بیاورد consists of two coordinated verbs describing the ordered actions. برفت is the classical past form of رفتن used here inside the subordinate clause introduced by تا (literary sequence: 'go/should go'); و simply coordinates the verbs; بیاورد is the verbal form meaning 'bring' (here used in the subordinate clause as the action to be performed). چندی modifies the following noun (‘a few/several’), and ردان is the direct object (the thing to be brought).
segments.s624-g19: بدو گفت شد is a typical old narrative sequence meaning 'it was said to him' / 'they told him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت شد = 'was said'). کار قیصر دراز is a predicate: کار (matter/affair) modified by قیصر in a genitive relation (کارِ قیصر), followed by دراز as a postpositive adjective meaning 'long/protracted' ('the Caesar's affair is protracted').
segments.s624-g20: رسولش is the subject ('his envoy'—رسول + enclitic possessive -ش). همی is a classical adverbial particle (here emphatic/continuative: 'still/indeed'); دیر with یابد forms a verbal phrase meaning 'to be long/delayed in (doing/obtaining) something'—دیر یابد literally 'will find it late' i.e. 'will be long delayed'. یابد is the verb 'find/obtain' (here 'obtain/secure'), and جواز is its object ('permission').
segments.s624-g21: چه introduces a question ('what/how'); مردست is the older orthographic/clitic form of مرد است ('he is a man' / 'what sort of man is he'), with ست = copula. و اندر خرد تا کجاست is a follow-up question phrase: اندر خرد = 'in wisdom', تا ... کجاست = 'to what extent/how far is he' (تا used idiomatically for 'to what extent').
segments.s624-g22: که is the relative 'who/that' linking to the description; دارد is the present/past-habitual 'has/possesses'; روان is the noun 'soul/spirit' (subject of the possessed relation); از خرد marks source/origin ('from wisdom'); پشت راست is a nominal phrase meaning literally 'a straight back' but here idiomatically 'a right/firm backing/support'—راست is predicate adjective 'upright/right' placed after the noun.
segments.s624-g23: بدو گفت is again the fixed phrase 'they/one said to him' or 'said to him'; موبد is the title 'mobed' (Zoroastrian priest) followed by the proper name انوشه بدی (here a compound/name). In older Persian titles often precede the name. In this line گفت functions as the reporting verb and the name phrase is the speaker.
segments.s624-g24: جهاندار is a predicative nominal meaning 'sovereign/ruler'. و با is simply 'and with'; فره ایزدی is a frozen phrase meaning 'divine farr' (فره = divine glory, ایزدی = divine), with با marking possession/association ('with/possessing the divine glory'). Together they describe the man as a ruler endowed with sacred radiance.
segments.s624-g49: This clause contains a time/sequence adverbial plus a verbal clause. وزان پس (from that time, then) sets the temporal sequence; به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with goodness' = 'safely/well'; فرستمش is a first-person past narrative form 'I sent him' with the enclitic object -ش; باز follows as an adverb 'back/again'. Word order is adverbial (time, manner) → verb + object → directional adverb.
segments.s624-g50: This short verbless clause gives the state/condition of the returned person. ز = 'from/of' (poetic preposition), مردم = 'the people', نیم here is unclear/elliptical in classical usage (it does not function as a separate verb), در جهان = 'in the world', بی‌نیاز = predicative adjective 'without need/self‑sufficient'. The whole unit describes being independent of people in the world.
segments.s624-g51: One (یکی, 'someone/a certain one') is the subject; رزم جوید is an older present/narrative form 'seeks/longs for battle' (جوید = 'seeks'); سپاه آورد is a simple past narrative clause 'he brought an army'. The two verbs are coordinated actions attributed to the same unspecified subject.
segments.s624-g52: دگر ('another') contrasts with one; بزم 'feast/banquet' is an object or end-state brought, و 'and' links the two objects, زرین کلاه 'golden helmet' is a compound noun (زرین modifying کلاه), and آورد is the past-tense verb 'he brought'. Word order: subject (implicit 'he/one') → objects → verb.
segments.s624-g53: The clause is best read as مرا (me) + را (object marker) + ارج ایشان (their honor/value) + بباید (impersonal modal 'it is necessary') + شناخت (verbal noun/infinitive 'to recognize/knowledge'). Grammatically, بباید governs شناخت and the whole expresses obligation: 'It is necessary that I recognize their worth' (i.e. 'I must recognize/esteem them').
segments.s624-g54: بزرگ is a predicate adjective 'great' (either exclamatory or predicative). آنک is the relative 'who/that', با + نامداران = 'with the renowned/illustrious ones', and بساخت is a past narrative verb form meaning roughly 'did/made/associated (much)' — here it denotes the action of associating or making friendships. The clause therefore praises the person who consorted with famous men.
segments.s624-g55: This clause opens with the imperative/particle برو ('go' or 'then go/so go on'), followed by آفرین کرد 'he made praise/expressed approval' (آفرین = interjection/noun 'praise', کرد = 'did/said'), موبد 'the mōbed/priest' is the agent, and به مهر 'with affection/with favour' is an adverbial phrase describing the manner. Verb is past narrative (کرد) with a preceding noun object (آفرین).
segments.s624-g56: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/that', شادان is an adjective 'joyful', بدی here is ambiguous in the manuscript (it could be an archaic verbal form or a noun meaning 'evil' but context requires a verb 'you were/gave joy'); تا 'until' introduces a temporal clause, بگردد is a verb 'it turns/becomes' (subjunctive/optative narrative) and سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the subject of بگردد. Overall the clause expresses that joy continued until the heavens turned.

sentences.s624-s5: He commanded that they make his robe of honour, and they paid out a precious treasure.
sentences.s624-s6: He said to him, 'May God be your refuge; may you be at the head of the sun's throne.'
sentences.s624-s7: On setting out, a pause of two weeks came upon him; the soft-bodied one of Khorasan fell into captivity.
sentences.s624-s8: When he had not yet arrived, a week passed; the king's heart was troubled by thought.
sentences.s624-s17: All his inferiors were meek as ewes in their behaviour, so that on the day of the hunt a hound would come before him.
sentences.s624-s18: Do not judge us by slowness or speed, and in this realm do not count one man against another.
sentences.s624-s19: Bahram Gūr said this to the mobed: that God grant (me) farr, dihim, and strength.
sentences.s624-s20: If the world-ruler made me victorious, he turned the dark night upon my fortune into day.
sentences.s624-s21: A certain Caesar of Rome, a Caesar of Fereydun's line, placed the crown upon his head.
sentences.s624-s22: His lineage is great — it goes back to Salm — and surpasses the kings in ceremony and in justice.
sentences.s624-s23: Now he made him beloved of the people and bestowed wisdom; he did not, like a khaghan, fall into madness.
sentences.s624-s24: We will call him forward before us at the time of the burden (occasion) of speech, to hear what he will say that may be of use.
sentences.s624-s13: There was an old man, with counsel and modesty; his speech was smooth and his voice soft.
sentences.s624-s14: There was one who had Plato in his hand as a master — wise, learned, and of noble birth.
sentences.s624-s15: Another, of noble manners, had come from Rome; now he stood astonished in this borderland and country.
sentences.s624-s16: He withered like a tulip in the month of Dey; his body was dry and his complexion the color of a reed.
sentences.s624-s1: One day the king said to Nersi thus: "From here, go with a gem and a hat."
sentences.s624-s2: I have given Khorasan to you; make it prosperous, and make the hearts of your subjects glad for us.
sentences.s624-s3: See to it that you are nothing but just; do not hang the harp among these passersby.
sentences.s624-s4: His father committed injustice and turned away from it, like a man stripped by the autumn wind.
sentences.s624-s9: He ordered that the chief of the mobeds go and bring several riders.
sentences.s624-s10: They told him that Caesar's affair had become protracted, and that his envoy would long be delayed in obtaining permission.
sentences.s624-s11: What sort of man is he, and how far does his wisdom reach? — One who has a soul whose backing is rightly from wisdom.
sentences.s624-s12: To him spoke the mobed Anushah-badi: 'A sovereign, and endowed with the divine farr (divine glory).'
sentences.s624-s25: From that time I sent him back in good condition, free of need among the people of the world.
sentences.s624-s26: One sought battle and brought an army; another brought feasting and a golden helmet.
sentences.s624-s27: Their worth must be acknowledged by me; great is he who associated with the renowned.
sentences.s624-s28: Go — the mobed offered praise with affection, so that you were joyful until the heavens turned.
